financiers.name: GLS Capital
financiers.desc: Funds commercial litigation, arbitration, and complex intellectual property matters.
financiers.criteria: $1M+ investment minimum. Commercial claims (contract, fraud, antitrust) plus a nationally ranked intellectual property practice — patent, trademark, copyright, and trade secret.

financiers.name: Bench Walk Advisors
financiers.desc: Team of litigators and investors credited with over $30B in settlements and judgments across funded matters; named Litigation Funder of the Year by Chambers & Partners in 2025.
financiers.criteria: $1M+ minimum, no stated maximum, at any stage. Broad mandate: commercial litigation, arbitration, competition, insolvency, class actions, mass torts, and enforcement/monetisation.

financiers.name: Curiam Capital
financiers.desc: Private investment firm focused on litigation financing for businesses and law firms; majority externally funded by investor Michael Platt.
financiers.criteria: $1M+ for single cases, $3M+ for portfolios (3+ cases required). Strong in patent and commercial IP; explicitly excludes matrimonial, medical malpractice, and individual personal injury claims.

financiers.name: LexShares
financiers.desc: Litigation finance firm operating both a dedicated fund and an online funding marketplace; typically invests $1M+ per matter, with mid-market deals from $200,000.
financiers.criteria: Marketplace tickets of $5K-$25K, sized to roughly 10% of expected case value. Commercial contract, IP, antitrust, and fiduciary duty claims, plus law firm and post-settlement funding.

financiers.name: Legalist
financiers.desc: Focuses on mid-market cases requiring under $1M in funding; manages over $110M in assets.
financiers.criteria: $50K-$5M per case, sourced via proprietary algorithmic court-record scanning. Broad commercial plaintiff and law firm funding at high volume — 250+ positions per fund.

financiers.name: Therium Capital Management
financiers.desc: One of the UK's founding litigation funders and an original Association of Litigation Funders member. In June 2025, its global litigation portfolio was taken over by Fortress Investment Group following internal restructuring — treat current standalone status with caution.
financiers.criteria: Historically funded securities, shareholder, arbitration, competition, IP, insolvency, and class actions. Portfolio now absorbed by Fortress — treat any current independent criteria with caution.
src: Inside the Fortress: Capital, Control, and the Quiet Collapse of Therium

financiers.name: Woodsford
financiers.desc: Association of Litigation Funders (England & Wales) member; active across commercial and group litigation funding.
financiers.criteria: Minimum claim size £5M, no stated maximum. Increasingly focused on large-scale group litigation and collective redress tied to corporate governance and ESG failures.

financiers.name: Augusta Ventures
financiers.desc: ALF member with a stated focus on infrastructure, construction, and energy disputes alongside general commercial funding.
financiers.criteria: Broad commercial, competition/antitrust, class action, and international arbitration mandate, with a notable infrastructure, construction, and energy practice plus financial-services claims.

financiers.name: Harbour Litigation Funding
financiers.desc: One of the longest-established members of the UK's Association of Litigation Funders.
financiers.criteria: No fixed minimum, but funds claims above roughly £10M as standard practice. Any commercial case type — the only stated exclusions are divorce and personal injury.

financiers.name: Litigation Capital Management (LCM)
financiers.desc: One of the longest-running dispute funders globally, with offices in Sydney, London, Singapore, Brisbane, and Melbourne. Funds single-case and portfolio matters, class actions, international arbitration, and insolvency-linked claims.
financiers.criteria: Single-case, portfolio, and claims-acquisition funding. Class actions, commercial claims, international arbitration, and insolvency-linked matters.

financiers.name: Deminor
financiers.desc: One of Europe's oldest litigation funders, originally focused on minority shareholder recovery. Helped secure two of Europe's largest securities settlements (Steinhoff, ~€1.4B; Fortis/Ageas, ~€1.3B); Band 1, Chambers Europe Litigation Support, 2024.
financiers.criteria: Minimum claim size around €1M, varying by jurisdiction. Particular depth in securities and shareholder claims, alongside commercial, arbitration, IP, and competition matters.

financiers.name: Innsworth
financiers.desc: Funds commercial litigation and arbitration claims above roughly $100M, including shareholder and class actions, insolvency claims, and investor-state arbitration. Formerly Bentham Europe, a joint venture of IMF Bentham and Elliott Management.
financiers.criteria: Minimum claim size roughly $100M — one of the highest thresholds in the market. Shareholder and class actions, insolvency claims, and investor-state arbitration only.

financiers.name: Balance Legal Capital
financiers.desc: Funds commercial disputes and class actions; team includes senior lawyers from major City of London firms.
financiers.criteria: Commercial disputes and class actions in the UK, Australia, and other common-law jurisdictions outside the US. Excludes divorce, individual personal injury, employment, and investment treaty arbitration.

financiers.name: Asertis
financiers.desc: Specializes in commercial dispute funding with an emphasis on group actions, enforcement, and insolvency; provides portfolio facilities to law firms.
financiers.criteria: Prefers commercial claims above roughly £4M (minimum £500K cost investment) and insolvency claims above £250K. Also group actions, securities litigation, and IP-adjacent disputes.

financiers.name: Certum Group

financiers.name: Arcadia Finance

financiers.criteria: Typically $2M-$25M per deal, as low as $500K in some cases. U.S. commercial and patent litigation, arbitration, mass torts, and law firm/patent-acquisition lending.

financiers.name: IVO Capital Partners

financiers.name: Statera Capital
financiers.desc: Deploys capital across breach of contract, business tort, fraud, IP, environmental, insolvency, unfair competition, and qui tam claims for the U.S. commercial middle market.

financiers.name: Pravati Capital
financiers.desc: Provides non-recourse capital advances, with a focus on mature personal injury and mass tort cases with an established settlement precedent, alongside law firm portfolio financing.
financiers.criteria: One of the few institutional funders active in personal injury and mass tort, provided the case is mature with an established settlement precedent; also law firm portfolio financing.

financiers.name: Delta Capital Partners Management
financiers.desc: Provides equity capital, credit, and bespoke financing for litigation finance, judgment enforcement, and asset recovery. CEO Christopher DeLise is a repeat Lawdragon 100 Global Leaders in Litigation Finance honoree.

financiers.name: Soryn IP Group
financiers.desc: Patent-focused finance and advisory firm founded by Michael Gulliford, a former Kirkland & Ellis patent litigator. Has closed more than $150M in patent-centric financings, including funding for universities, law firms, and NPEs pursuing enforcement.
financiers.criteria: IP-only: patent, trademark, and other IP-backed financing for companies, universities, law firms, and NPEs. Over $150M in closed patent-centric financings to date.

financiers.name: Lake Whillans
financiers.desc: Commercial litigation finance firm; frequently cited in commentary on early landmark U.S. funding disputes, including Miller UK v. Caterpillar.
financiers.criteria: Roughly $1-15M per single case, generally targeting likely damages above $15M (about a 10:1 damages-to-investment ratio). General commercial litigation.

financiers.name: Litigation Lending Services

financiers.criteria: Broad commercial, insolvency, and class action funding in Australia and New Zealand; as a rule of thumb, wants claims of at least roughly $500K.

financiers.name: Lumius Capital
financiers.desc: Listed in the third-party-funding.org global directory of litigation and arbitration funders.
financiers.criteria: Commercial litigation and law firm funding based in the New York area; specific published investment criteria not available.

disputes.name: Glaz LLC (Burford affiliates) v. Sysco Corp.
disputes.citation: LCIA Case No. 225609; Glaz LLC v. Sysco, 1:23-cv-02489 (S.D.N.Y.)
disputes.background: Burford invested $140M+ in Sysco's protein price-fixing antitrust claims under a Capital Provision Agreement. When Sysco moved to settle at levels Burford deemed too low, Burford commenced LCIA arbitration and obtained an order barring Sysco from settling; enforcement litigation followed in federal court.
disputes.holding: Resolved by settlement: Sysco assigned its antitrust claims to Burford SPV Carina Ventures LLC, and the arbitration was dismissed by stipulation in June 2023.
disputes.lesson: 'Passive capital' language can coexist with powerful settlement-consent covenants. Every CPA should state with precision when, whether, and how a funder may block or compel settlement — and what dispute mechanism governs.

disputes.name: In re Pork Antitrust Litig. (Sysco/Carina substitution)
disputes.court: U.S. District Court, D. Minn. (Judge Tunheim)
disputes.posture: Motion to substitute funder SPV as plaintiff
disputes.background: After the Sysco-Burford settlement, Sysco and Carina asked the Minnesota MDL court to substitute Carina as plaintiff in the pork and beef antitrust actions.
disputes.holding: Substitution denied. The court held the assignment did violence to Rule 25(c)'s meaning of transferring an 'interest,' had caused serious disruption, and that the pro-settlement rationale told 'only half' the story; a Chicago judge reached the opposite result in the Broilers case weeks later.
disputes.lesson: Judges disagree sharply on whether a funder may step into its client's shoes as named plaintiff. Assignment-based exits are jurisdiction-sensitive and can strand a funder's investment.

disputes.name: Carina Ventures LLC v. Pilgrim's Pride Corp.
disputes.court: U.S. Court of Appeals, Seventh Circuit
disputes.posture: Funder SPV (as assignee-plaintiff) vs. settling defendant
disputes.background: Pilgrim's Pride argued a $50M settlement reached with Sysco in Dec. 2022 (before the Carina assignment) was binding. The district court enforced it; Carina appealed.
disputes.holding: Seventh Circuit reversed on Illinois contract-formation grounds — material terms were not definite and drafts stated they bound no one until signed. Judge Maldonado concurred separately to condemn Burford for 'having turned the courtroom into a trading floor' and called the case 'a cautionary tale' for funded parties.
disputes.lesson: Even a funder win can generate judicial language that becomes Exhibit A for reform advocates. Settlement-in-principle timing versus assignment timing must be documented meticulously; unsigned term sheets are a litigation risk in both directions.

disputes.name: Miller UK Ltd. v. Caterpillar, Inc.
disputes.posture: Defendant sought funding documents in discovery
disputes.background: Caterpillar sought Miller's litigation funding documents, arguing champerty and relevance. The leading early U.S. opinion on funding discovery.
disputes.holding: Court rejected the champerty attack and held materials shared with funders under NDA retained work-product protection, though the funding agreement itself was not privileged as a communication.
disputes.lesson: Use NDAs and common-interest frameworks before sharing evaluative materials with funders; assume the agreement itself may be discoverable even where deal communications are protected.

disputes.name: Viamedia, Inc. v. Comcast Corp.
disputes.background: Comcast sought documents Viamedia shared with prospective litigation funders.
disputes.holding: Work-product protection held not waived by disclosure to funders; funding discovery largely denied.
disputes.lesson: Courts in the N.D. Ill. line consistently protect funder communications made under confidentiality — replicate that structure in every deal.

disputes.name: Fulton v. Foley
disputes.background: Defendants sought litigation funding information from a funded plaintiff.
disputes.holding: Funding discovery denied as irrelevant absent a particularized showing.
disputes.lesson: The majority U.S. rule: no funding discovery without case-specific relevance — but the exception now has teeth (see Lituma).

disputes.name: Art Akiane LLC v. Art & SoulWorks LLC
disputes.background: Defendant sought funding-related discovery in an IP dispute.
disputes.holding: Court found funding discovery irrelevant and potentially harmful to plaintiff; denied.
disputes.lesson: Relevance, not privilege, is often the cleanest shield for funding materials.

disputes.name: Carlyle Investment Mgmt. v. Moonmouth Co.
disputes.background: Dispute over production of litigation funding agreement and related documents.
disputes.holding: Chancery held funding documents protected work product — prepared because of litigation.
disputes.lesson: Delaware extends work-product protection to funding deal documents; papering deals 'because of' litigation matters to the analysis.

disputes.name: In re National Prescription Opiate Litigation
disputes.background: Judge Polster ordered plaintiffs' counsel to disclose third-party funding in camera and certify the funder had no control over strategy or settlement.
disputes.holding: First major MDL-wide funding disclosure order; in camera model followed elsewhere.
disputes.lesson: The in-camera + no-control certification model is the template many courts now use; funders should draft agreements able to survive that certification.

disputes.name: Gbarabe v. Chevron Corp.
disputes.background: In a Nigerian villagers' class action, Chevron sought the funding agreement as relevant to adequacy of class counsel.
disputes.holding: Disclosure of the funding agreement ordered — adequacy in class actions can trump confidentiality.
disputes.lesson: Class actions are the weak point of funding confidentiality; ND Cal standing order now requires funding disclosure in all class actions.

disputes.name: Nimitz Technologies LLC v. CNET Media, Inc.
disputes.court: U.S. District Court, D. Del. (Chief Judge Connolly); Fed. Cir.
disputes.citation: No. 21-1247 (D. Del.); In re Nimitz Techs., 2022 WL 17494845 (Fed. Cir. 2022)
disputes.background: Chief Judge Connolly's standing order requires disclosure of third-party funding; his inquiry into shell-entity patent plaintiffs (Nimitz) probed who really owned and financed the claims.
disputes.holding: Federal Circuit denied mandamus, leaving the disclosure inquiry intact; the episode exposed hidden funding structures behind NPE suits.
disputes.lesson: Patent monetization structures relying on opacity are increasingly untenable in Delaware; funders should expect judicial tracing of beneficial ownership.

disputes.name: In re Valsartan NDMA Products Liability Litig.
disputes.background: Defendants sought broad discovery of plaintiffs' litigation funding in the MDL.
disputes.holding: Denied — funding was irrelevant absent specific showing; NJ later adopted L. Civ. R. 7.1.1 (2021) requiring baseline funding disclosure statements.
disputes.lesson: Even disclosure-rule districts distinguish baseline identification from merits discovery into funding terms.

disputes.name: Lituma v. Liberty Coca-Cola Beverages LLC
disputes.posture: Defense sought funding discovery on fraud showing
disputes.background: In a personal injury action, defendants presented evidence of systemic staged-accident fraud and sought discovery into plaintiffs' litigation funding.
disputes.holding: First Department affirmed vacatur of note of issue and ordered funding discovery — fraud allegations opened the door.
disputes.lesson: The fraud exception to funding-discovery denial is now established in NY; funders in the PI space need underwriting controls against fraud rings, which also drove NY's 2026 Consumer Litigation Funding Act.

disputes.name: Maslowski v. Prospect Funding Partners LLC
disputes.posture: Funder sued to enforce consumer funding agreement
disputes.background: A personal-injury plaintiff received a purchase agreement advance; when she resisted repayment, litigation tested Minnesota's common-law champerty bar.
disputes.holding: Minnesota Supreme Court abolished common-law champerty prospectively, citing modern safeguards; on remand courts still scrutinized the agreement's terms (e.g., rates) for unconscionability.
disputes.lesson: Champerty's decline does not end scrutiny — unconscionability and rate review survive as the second line of attack on consumer agreements.

disputes.name: Boling v. Prospect Funding Holdings, LLC
disputes.court: U.S. Court of Appeals, Sixth Circuit (Kentucky law)
disputes.background: Prospect sued to enforce pre-settlement advances against a Kentucky PI plaintiff.
disputes.holding: Applying Kentucky law, the court held the funding agreements champertous and void as against public policy.
disputes.lesson: State-by-state champerty mapping is existential for consumer funders; identical contracts are enforceable in one state and void next door.

disputes.name: Justinian Capital SPC v. WestLB AG
disputes.background: Justinian acquired distressed notes for the sole purpose of suing WestLB, with no genuine obligation to pay the purchase price.
disputes.holding: Held champertous under N.Y. Jud. Law § 489; sham purchase fell outside the statute's safe harbor.
disputes.lesson: New York's champerty statute is alive for claim acquisitions: real consideration, genuinely at risk, is what separates lawful claim purchases from champerty.

disputes.name: Phoenix Light SF Ltd. v. U.S. Bank N.A.
disputes.background: Assignments of rights to sue under CDO trust certificates, executed mid-litigation to cure standing problems.
disputes.holding: Held champertous under Justinian — assignments made for the purpose of litigation violated § 489.
disputes.lesson: Standing 'fixes' by assignment during litigation are a champerty trap in New York.

disputes.name: Osprey, Inc. v. Cabana Limited Partnership
disputes.background: Assignment of an interest in a real-estate lawsuit in exchange for funding was attacked as champertous.
disputes.holding: South Carolina abolished champerty as a defense, holding other doctrines police litigation abuse.
disputes.lesson: One of the watershed pro-funding decisions; frequently cited by courts retiring champerty.

disputes.name: Saladini v. Righellis
disputes.posture: Funder sought recovery from funded claimant
disputes.background: An individual advanced funds for a lawsuit in exchange for a share of recovery; enforceability turned on champerty.
disputes.holding: SJC abandoned champerty in Massachusetts, reviewing such agreements instead for overall fairness.
disputes.lesson: The 'fairness review' framework Saladini adopted is the model many courts now apply in champerty's place.

disputes.name: Anglo-Dutch Petroleum Int'l v. Haskell
disputes.background: After winning its underlying case, Anglo-Dutch resisted paying funders, arguing the agreements were usurious loans and champertous.
disputes.holding: Court enforced the agreements — not loans (repayment contingent), not champertous, not against Texas public policy.
disputes.lesson: The contingent-repayment structure is what keeps commercial funding outside usury statutes; funded parties' 'buyer's remorse' defenses usually fail in Texas.

disputes.name: Wilson v. Harris
disputes.background: Funding agreement for a share of lawsuit proceeds challenged under Alabama public policy.
disputes.holding: Void — a 'gambling contract' akin to champerty.
disputes.lesson: Alabama remains one of the most hostile jurisdictions; some contracts are unsalvageable by drafting.

disputes.name: WFIC, LLC v. LaBarre
disputes.posture: Funding tied to counsel's fee recovery
disputes.background: A funding arrangement connected to a lawyer's fee interest was challenged.
disputes.holding: Champerty 'remains a viable defense in Pennsylvania'; agreement invalidated.
disputes.lesson: Pennsylvania is a live champerty jurisdiction, especially for attorney-side funding structures.

disputes.name: Charge Injection Technologies, Inc. v. E.I. DuPont
disputes.posture: Defendant attacked plaintiff's funding as champertous
disputes.background: DuPont argued CIT's funding agreement with Burford was champertous/maintenance and stripped CIT of real-party-in-interest status.
disputes.holding: Rejected — funder had no control of the litigation and no assignment of the claim; agreement lawful in Delaware.
disputes.lesson: The control/assignment line is the key champerty firewall in commercial funding: passive, non-controlling capital survives.

disputes.name: Oasis Legal Finance Group, LLC v. Coffman
disputes.background: Funders sought a ruling that pre-settlement advances were not 'loans' under Colorado's consumer credit code.
disputes.holding: Held: the transactions are loans subject to the UCCC, despite contingent repayment.
disputes.lesson: The loan-vs-asset-purchase characterization battle is the core regulatory risk in consumer funding, and states split on it (contrast Ruth v. Cherokee).

disputes.name: Ruth v. Cherokee Funding, LLC
disputes.background: Consumers argued their funding agreements violated Georgia's Payday Lending and Industrial Loan Acts.
disputes.holding: Held: contingent, non-recourse funding agreements are not 'loans' under those statutes.
disputes.lesson: Mirror image of Coffman — the same product is a loan in Colorado and not in Georgia. (Note: Georgia adopted a funding statute in 2025, changing the landscape.)

disputes.name: Echeverria v. Estate of Lindner
disputes.background: A funding advance was challenged as a usurious loan where recovery on the underlying claim was effectively certain.
disputes.holding: Court treated the advance as a loan subject to usury limits because repayment risk was illusory.
disputes.lesson: Non-recourse labels don't control; if the funder bears no real risk, usury law can apply. Risk-of-loss analysis should be documented at underwriting.

disputes.name: CFPB & NY AG v. RD Legal Funding, LLC
disputes.court: U.S. District Court, S.D.N.Y.; Second Circuit
disputes.background: Regulators alleged RD Legal's advances to NFL concussion class members and 9/11 first responders were deceptive, disguised usurious loans; the case also became a vehicle for the CFPB constitutionality fight.
disputes.holding: Advances recharacterized as loans; claims largely sustained after years of appellate detours over CFPB structure.
disputes.lesson: Regulatory recharacterization risk is highest where funded claimants are sympathetic populations with near-certain recoveries; assignments of settlement proceeds may also be void under anti-assignment rules.

disputes.name: Hamilton Capital VII, LLC v. Khorrami, LLP
disputes.background: A law firm resisted repaying a credit facility secured by its receivables, arguing the arrangement was impermissible fee-splitting with a non-lawyer.
disputes.holding: Enforced — a loan secured by receivables is not fee-splitting; public policy supports law firm access to capital.
disputes.lesson: Law-firm portfolio lending structured as secured credit (not profit shares) has survived Rule 5.4 attacks in NY courts — though NYC Bar Op. 2018-5 later criticized proceeds-contingent versions.

disputes.name: Chevron Corp. v. Donziger
disputes.court: U.S. District Court, S.D.N.Y.; Second Circuit
disputes.funders: Burford Capital and others (Treca facility) funded the Lago Agrio claimants
disputes.posture: Defendant company's RICO action against claimant team
disputes.background: Chevron's RICO case over the $9.5B Ecuadorian judgment exposed the funding arrangements behind the Lago Agrio litigation; Burford later stated it had been defrauded by the claimant team and exited.
disputes.holding: Judgment held procured by fraud and unenforceable in the U.S.; the funding agreement's terms became public evidence.
disputes.lesson: Funder diligence must extend to the integrity of the funded team, not just claim merits; funding agreements can become trial exhibits.

disputes.name: Arkin v. Borchard Lines Ltd.
disputes.posture: Costs liability of funder after funded claim failed
disputes.background: A funded competition claim failed; the question was the funder's exposure to the winner's costs.
disputes.holding: Created the 'Arkin cap': a commercial funder's adverse-costs liability limited to the amount of its funding.
disputes.lesson: The cap made UK commercial funding economically viable — but it is a guideline, not a rule (see Davey).

disputes.name: Excalibur Ventures LLC v. Texas Keystone Inc.
disputes.posture: Costs orders against funders of a failed claim
disputes.background: Funders bankrolled a speculative $1.6B claim that failed spectacularly, with the court condemning how it was pursued.
disputes.holding: Funders held jointly liable for indemnity-basis costs — they 'follow the fortunes' of the litigation they fund.
disputes.lesson: Funders inherit the litigation conduct of those they finance; diligence and monitoring are costs-exposure management, not just underwriting.

disputes.name: R (PACCAR Inc.) v. Competition Appeal Tribunal
disputes.status: Decided; largely mitigated by later cases
disputes.background: The Supreme Court held LFAs paying funders a percentage of damages are 'damages-based agreements' — unenforceable in opt-out CAT proceedings unless DBA-compliant.
disputes.holding: Threw hundreds of UK funding agreements into doubt overnight; funders rewrote LFAs around multiples of outlay.
disputes.lesson: Regulatory characterization risk can strike an entire market at once. Post-PACCAR: Court of Appeal upheld revised/'future-proofed' LFAs in Sony v. Neill (2025), and the Supreme Court refused further appeal (Nov. 2025).

disputes.name: Gutmann v. Apple Inc. (and Rodger v. Google, CAT)
disputes.court: England & Wales Court of Appeal; CAT
disputes.posture: Funder payment priority in collective proceedings
disputes.background: Whether funders and lawyers can be paid from damages before class members, and how tribunals assess funder returns.
disputes.holding: Yes — the CAT may order funder/lawyer payment ahead of the class; Rodger v. Google added that paying the funder first can even serve the class's interests. Merricks settlement scrutiny showed the CAT will also police the size of returns.
disputes.lesson: UK funder economics are now openly supervised: priority is available, but returns must be justified with evidence.

disputes.name: Therium Litigation Funding A IC v. Bugsby Property LLC
disputes.posture: Funder vs. funded party over settlement proceeds
disputes.background: Post-PACCAR, a funded party argued its LFA was unenforceable and resisted paying Therium from settlement proceeds.
disputes.holding: Court granted Therium a proprietary injunction preserving proceeds, finding serious issues to be tried in the funder's favor.
disputes.lesson: Funders have real remedies when funded parties weaponize PACCAR; proceeds-tracing and security provisions in LFAs proved their worth.

disputes.name: Essar Oilfields Services Ltd. v. Norscot Rig Mgmt.
disputes.posture: Recoverability of funding costs in arbitration
disputes.background: An ICC tribunal awarded the winning party the cost of its litigation funding (including the funder's uplift) as 'other costs'; the loser challenged the award.
disputes.holding: Upheld — arbitrators may award third-party funding costs under the Arbitration Act.
disputes.lesson: In arbitration, funding costs can shift to the loser — a major economic asymmetry vs. court litigation that parties should price into strategy.

disputes.name: Petersen Energia v. Argentine Republic (YPF)
disputes.court: U.S. District Court, S.D.N.Y.; enforcement worldwide
disputes.funders: Burford Capital (acquired claims out of Spanish insolvency)
disputes.background: Burford acquired Petersen's claims through Spanish insolvency proceedings and funded the decade-long YPF expropriation case; Argentina has attacked Burford's role and returns throughout enforcement.
disputes.holding: $16.1B judgment for plaintiffs — the largest funder-driven outcome to date; Burford's share (a large majority of proceeds) is itself a public flashpoint; collection remains contested across jurisdictions.
disputes.lesson: Insolvency estates are a major funder acquisition channel, and mega-returns invite sovereign-level resistance and political scrutiny of the industry.

disputes.name: Houle v. St. Jude Medical Inc.
disputes.background: Ontario courts reviewed a proposed class-action funding agreement; the first version was rejected as champertous (excessive funder compensation/control), then approved after renegotiation.
disputes.holding: LFAs in Ontario class actions require court approval; courts police funder returns, control, and privilege safeguards.
disputes.lesson: Court-approval regimes (Canada, and analogously the CAT) preview where U.S. class-action funding oversight may head.

disputes.name: 2770095 Ontario Inc. v. Morgan
disputes.posture: Champerty attack on assigned fraud claims
disputes.background: A foreign entity assigned fraud claims to an Ontario shell created to litigate them, keeping ~3% for the assignor and ceding all control.
disputes.holding: Action dismissed as champertous 'trafficking in litigation' — despite a strong prima facie fraud case.
disputes.lesson: Even pro-funding jurisdictions draw the line at claim-trafficking structures with nominal assignor interest and total control transfer.
